cases.title: PayPal pays users $10 each to drive viral growth
cases.title_zh: PayPal 给每位用户 10 美元驱动病毒式增长
cases.before_state: PayPal's email payment product worked well but growth was slow and expenses mounted. Advertising was too expensive. Deals with big banks kept falling through. They needed at least a million users for the business to work.
cases.before_state_zh: PayPal 的邮件支付产品运行良好，但增长缓慢、成本攀升。广告太贵，与大银行的合作屡屡失败。他们至少需要百万用户才能让业务运转。
cases.trigger: Conventional customer acquisition channels all failed. The team decided to try something radical: directly paying people to sign up.
cases.trigger_zh: 传统获客渠道全部失效。团队决定尝试激进做法：直接付钱让人注册。
cases.action: PayPal gave new customers $10 for joining and $10 more for every friend they referred. This created an exponential growth loop — each new user brought more users. The cost structure was unsustainable on its own, but with a large user base, PayPal had a clear path to profitability through transaction fees.
cases.action_zh: PayPal 给新用户 10 美元注册奖励，每推荐一位朋友再给 10 美元。这形成了指数增长闭环——每个新用户带来更多用户。单看成本结构不可持续，但有了庞大用户群，PayPal 可通过交易费实现盈利。
cases.result: They achieved 7% daily growth, with the user base nearly doubling every 10 days. Within four or five months they had hundreds of thousands of users.
cases.result_zh: 他们实现了 7% 的日增长，用户基数每 10 天几乎翻倍。四五个月内就达到数十万用户。
cases.after_state: The $20-per-customer acquisition cost was far exceeded by the lifetime transaction fees. This viral strategy became a foundational case study in growth hacking.
cases.after_state_zh: 每用户 20 美元的获客成本远低于终身交易费收入。这一病毒策略成为增长黑客的经典案例。
hard_numbers_zh: 10 美元 — 新用户注册奖励
10 美元 — 每推荐一位朋友的奖励
7% — 日增长率
10 天 — 用户基数翻倍周期
20 美元 — 单用户总获客成本
cases.source_excerpt: We gave new customers $10 for joining, and we gave them $10 more every time they referred a friend. This got us hundreds of thousands of new customers and an exponential growth rate.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 我们给新用户 10 美元注册奖励，每推荐一位朋友再给 10 美元。这为我们带来数十万新用户和指数级增长率。

cases.title: PayPal races to raise $100M before the dot-com crash
cases.title_zh: PayPal 在互联网泡沫崩盘前紧急融资 1 亿美元
cases.before_state: Late 1999. Dot-com mania at peak. PayPal growing fast but unprofitable. Thiel was scared — not because he doubted PayPal, but because everyone else in the Valley seemed ready to believe anything.
cases.before_state_zh: 1999 年末，互联网泡沫达到顶峰。PayPal 增长很快但尚未盈利。Thiel 很害怕——不是怀疑 PayPal，而是硅谷其他人似乎什么都愿意相信。
cases.trigger: On February 16, 2000, the Wall Street Journal praised PayPal's viral growth and suggested it was worth $500 million. Thiel knew the crash was imminent and moved to raise capital while the window was open.
cases.trigger_zh: 2000 年 2 月 16 日，《华尔街日报》盛赞 PayPal 的病毒式增长，估值 5 亿美元。Thiel 知道崩盘将至，趁窗口还在抓紧融资。
cases.action: PayPal raised $100 million the next month. Their lead investor took the Journal's valuation as authoritative. A South Korean firm even wired $5 million without negotiating a deal or signing documents — when Thiel tried to return the money, they wouldn't tell him where to send it.
cases.action_zh: PayPal 次月融资 1 亿美元。领投方把日报估值当权威。一家韩国公司甚至未经谈判或签约就汇来 500 万美元——Thiel 想退款时，对方不肯告知汇款地址。
cases.result: The March 2000 financing round bought them the time to make PayPal a success. Just as they closed the deal, the bubble popped. The NASDAQ peaked at 5,048 in mid-March and crashed to 3,321 by mid-April.
cases.result_zh: 2000 年 3 月的融资为他们争取到成功的时间。刚签完约，泡沫就破了。纳斯达克 3 月中旬见顶 5048 点，4 月跌至 3321。
cases.after_state: PayPal survived because they had cash reserves. By October 2002, the NASDAQ bottomed at 1,114. PayPal was acquired by eBay for $1.5 billion that same year.
cases.after_state_zh: PayPal 因有现金储备活了下来。2002 年 10 月纳斯达克触底 1114 点。同年 PayPal 被 eBay 以 15 亿美元收购。
hard_numbers: $5 million — unsolicited Korean investment, no paperwork
hard_numbers_zh: 5 亿美元 — 《华尔街日报》对 PayPal 估值
1 亿美元 — 2000 年 3 月完成的融资
500 万美元 — 韩国公司主动投资，无书面协议
5048 — 2000 年 3 月中旬纳斯达克峰值
3321 — 4 月纳斯达克崩盘后点位
1114 — 2002 年 10 月纳斯达克底部
15 亿美元 — 2002 年 eBay 收购 PayPal

quotes.text: I was scared out of my wits—not because I didn't believe in our company, but because it seemed like everyone else in the Valley was ready to believe anything at all.
quotes.text_zh: 我吓得魂不附体——不是不相信自己的公司，而是硅谷其他人似乎什么都愿意相信。
quotes.speaker: Peter Thiel

cases.source_excerpt: When I was running PayPal in late 1999, I was scared out of my wits—not because I didn't believe in our company, but because it seemed like everyone else in the Valley was ready to believe anything at all... we moved fast to raise funds while we could.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 1999 年末经营 PayPal 时，我吓得魂不附体——不是不相信自己的公司，而是硅谷其他人似乎什么都愿意相信……我们趁还能融资时抓紧行动。

cases.title: Google disguises its monopoly by redefining its market
cases.title_zh: Google 通过重新定义市场来掩盖垄断
cases.before_state: Google owned about 68% of the search market. 95% of its revenue came from search advertising. But publicly, the company framed itself as just another player in a vast market.
cases.before_state_zh: Google 占据约 68% 的搜索市场，95% 收入来自搜索广告。但公开场合，公司把自己包装成广阔市场中的普通玩家。
cases.trigger: At a 2011 congressional hearing, Google chairman Eric Schmidt described Google's competitive landscape by defining the market as 'technology' broadly — not 'search.'
cases.trigger_zh: 2011 年国会听证会上，Google 董事长埃里克·施密特把市场定义为广义「科技」而非「搜索」，来描述竞争格局。
cases.action: By framing their market as the union of search + mobile phones + wearable computers + self-driving cars, Google made its dominance seem trivial. In global consumer tech ($964 billion), Google's non-search revenue of $2.35 billion was less than 0.24% of the market.
cases.action_zh: 把市场定义为搜索+手机+可穿戴+自动驾驶的并集，Google 让自身主导地位显得微不足道。在全球消费科技 9640 亿美元市场中，Google 非搜索收入 23.5 亿美元占比不到 0.24%。
cases.result: The framing helped Google avoid serious antitrust action for years. Schmidt could tell Congress with a straight face: 'We face an extremely competitive landscape.'
cases.result_zh: 这一话术帮 Google 多年规避反垄断重拳。施密特可以一本正经告诉国会：「我们面临极其激烈的竞争。」
cases.after_state: Thiel used Google as the prime example of how monopolists disguise their power by expanding market definitions, while competitive businesses narrow theirs to feel unique.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 以 Google 为例说明：垄断者通过扩大市场定义来掩饰权力，而竞争型企业则缩小定义以显得独特。
hard_numbers: 0.24% — Google's share when market is redefined
hard_numbers_zh: 68% — Google 搜索市场份额
95% — 来自搜索广告的收入
23.5 亿美元 — 2012 年 Google 非搜索收入
9640 亿美元 — 全球消费科技市场
0.24% — 重新定义市场后 Google 的份额

quotes.text: We face an extremely competitive landscape in which consumers have a multitude of options to access information.
quotes.text_zh: 我们面临极其激烈的竞争，消费者有多种获取信息的渠道。
quotes.speaker: Eric Schmidt

cases.source_excerpt: 95% of Google's revenue comes from search advertising; its other products generated just $2.35 billion in 2012... Since consumer tech is a $964 billion market globally, Google owns less than 0.24% of it.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: Google 95% 收入来自搜索广告；2012 年其他产品仅贡献 23.5 亿美元……全球消费科技市场 9640 亿美元，Google 占比不到 0.24%。

cases.title: The restaurant that thinks its naan is a competitive moat
cases.title_zh: 以为馕饼是护城河的餐厅
cases.before_state: Castro Street in Mountain View was packed with dozens of restaurants — Indian, sushi, burgers — all competing fiercely on tiny differences.
cases.before_state_zh: 山景城 Castro 街挤满数十家餐厅——印度菜、寿司、汉堡——都在微小差异上激烈竞争。
cases.trigger: Thiel observed that restaurant owners claim unique positioning based on trivial differentiation. A British food restaurant in Palo Alto might say 'no one else is doing it' — technically true, but the real market is all restaurants.
cases.trigger_zh: Thiel 观察到，餐厅老板用琐碎差异宣称独特定位。帕洛阿尔托的英式餐厅可能说「没人做这个」——技术上没错，但真实市场是所有餐厅。
cases.action: Non-monopolists exaggerate their distinction by defining their market as the intersection of smaller markets: British food + restaurant + Palo Alto. Meanwhile, PayPal — with fewer employees than the Castro Street restaurants — was worth more than all of them combined because it was the only email-based payments company in the world.
cases.action_zh: 非垄断者把小市场交集当定位：英式菜+餐厅+帕洛阿尔托。而 PayPal 员工比 Castro 街餐厅还少，却比它们加起来更值钱，因为它是全球唯一的邮件支付公司。
cases.result: Restaurants fail at high rates because the framing trick works on psychology, not on markets.
cases.result_zh: 餐厅倒闭率高，因为话术只骗得了心理，骗不了市场。
cases.after_state: Thiel created his signature framework: monopolists describe their market as the union of large markets (to look small), while competitive businesses describe theirs as the intersection of small markets (to feel unique).
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 总结框架：垄断者用大市场并集描述自己（显得小），竞争型企业用小市场交集描述自己（显得独特）。

quotes.text: If you lose sight of competitive reality and focus on trivial differentiating factors—maybe you think your naan is superior because of your great-grandmother's recipe—your business is unlikely to survive.
quotes.text_zh: 若忽视竞争现实、沉迷琐碎差异——比如觉得曾祖母的馕饼配方是护城河——你的生意很难活下去。
quotes.speaker: Peter Thiel

concepts_zh: 交集 vs 并集话术
cases.source_excerpt: Suppose you want to start a restaurant that serves British food in Palo Alto. 'No one else is doing it,' you might reason. 'We'll own the entire market.' But that's only true if the relevant market is the market for British food specifically.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 假设你想在帕洛阿尔托开一家英式餐厅。「没人做这个，」你会想，「我们独占整个市场。」但这只有在相关市场是「英式菜」时才成立。

cases.title: Three-star Michelin chef kills himself over competition
cases.title_zh: 三星米其林主厨因竞争自杀
cases.before_state: Bernard Loiseau held three Michelin stars, the highest rating in French dining. The restaurant industry's culture of intense competition drove chefs to extreme stress.
cases.before_state_zh: 伯纳德·卢瓦索拥有三星米其林，法国餐饮最高荣誉。餐饮业激烈竞争的文化让主厨们承受极大压力。
cases.trigger: A competing French dining guide downgraded Loiseau's restaurant. Loiseau was quoted as saying 'If I lose a star, I will commit suicide.'
cases.trigger_zh: 另一家法国餐饮指南下调了卢瓦索餐厅的评级。卢瓦索曾说过：「如果我掉星，我就自杀。」
cases.action: Michelin maintained his three-star rating. But the downgrade by the competing guide was enough. Loiseau killed himself in 2003.
cases.action_zh: 米其林维持了他的三星。但竞争对手的降级就够了。卢瓦索 2003 年自杀。
cases.result: His death became a stark symbol of how competitive ecosystems push people toward ruthlessness or destruction.
cases.result_zh: 他的死成为竞争生态如何把人推向冷酷或毁灭的鲜明象征。
cases.after_state: Thiel used this as his most extreme illustration of how perfect competition is not healthy but destructive — contrasted with Google, which as a monopoly can afford to think about things other than survival.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 用此极端案例说明：完全竞争不是健康而是毁灭——对比 Google 作为垄断者可以思考生存以外的事。
hard_numbers_zh: 3 — 卢瓦索拥有的米其林星
2003 — 去世年份

quotes.text: If I lose a star, I will commit suicide.
quotes.speaker: Bernard Loiseau

cases.source_excerpt: French chef and winner of three Michelin stars Bernard Loiseau was quoted as saying, 'If I lose a star, I will commit suicide.' Michelin maintained his rating, but Loiseau killed himself anyway in 2003 when a competing French dining guide downgraded his restaurant.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 三星米其林主厨伯纳德·卢瓦索曾说：「如果我掉星，我就自杀。」米其林维持了评级，但 2003 年另一家法国餐饮指南降级后，卢瓦索还是自杀了。

cases.title: Microsoft vs Google war lets Apple surpass them both
cases.title_zh: 微软与 Google 的战争让苹果超越两者
cases.before_state: Microsoft built OS + Office. Google built Search. As startups, each had been content to leave the other alone. Their businesses were entirely separate.
cases.before_state_zh: 微软做 OS+Office，Google 做搜索。创业期各自相安无事，业务完全分离。
cases.trigger: As both grew, they began to see each other as threats. Google launched Chrome OS and Docs. Microsoft launched Bing.
cases.trigger_zh: 两家壮大后开始视对方为威胁。Google 推出 Chrome OS 和 Docs，微软推出 Bing。
cases.action: A decade of war: Windows vs Chrome OS, Bing vs Google Search, Explorer vs Chrome, Office vs Docs, Surface vs Nexus. Meanwhile, Apple stayed focused on building distinct products instead of fighting competitors.
cases.action_zh: 十年战争：Windows vs Chrome OS、Bing vs Google、Explorer vs Chrome、Office vs Docs、Surface vs Nexus。而苹果专注做差异化产品，而非与对手缠斗。
cases.result: In January 2013, Apple's market cap was $500 billion, while Google and Microsoft combined were worth $467 billion. Just three years before, each had been individually more valuable than Apple.
cases.result_zh: 2013 年 1 月，苹果市值 5000 亿美元，Google 和微软合计 4670 亿。三年前各自都比苹果值钱。
cases.after_state: Thiel's Shakespearean parable: like Montagues and Capulets, the two houses destroyed value by fighting. The winner was the company that refused to compete.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 的莎士比亚寓言：像蒙太古与凯普莱特，两大家族在争斗中毁灭价值。赢家是拒绝竞争的那家。
hard_numbers_zh: 5000 亿美元 — 2013 年 1 月苹果市值
4670 亿美元 — 2013 年 1 月 Google+微软合计

quotes.text: Competition can make people hallucinate opportunities where none exist.
quotes.text_zh: 竞争会让人在不存在机会的地方产生幻觉。
quotes.speaker: Peter Thiel

concepts_zh: 莎士比亚蒙太古-凯普莱特模型
cases.source_excerpt: Just as war cost the Montagues and Capulets their children, it cost Microsoft and Google their dominance: Apple came along and overtook them all. In January 2013, Apple's market capitalization was $500 billion, while Google and Microsoft combined were worth $467 billion.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 正如战争让蒙太古与凯普莱特失去子女，战争让微软和 Google 失去主导：苹果乘势超越两者。2013 年 1 月苹果市值 5000 亿美元，Google 与微软合计 4670 亿。

cases.title: Oracle CEO sends ice cream trucks to raid rival's employees
cases.title_zh: 甲骨文 CEO 派冰淇淋车挖对手员工
cases.before_state: Tom Siebel was Larry Ellison's top salesman at Oracle before leaving to found Siebel Systems in 1993. Ellison was livid. Both men were hard-charging Chicagoans who hated to lose.
cases.before_state_zh: 汤姆·西贝尔曾是拉里·埃里森在甲骨文的顶级销售，1993 年离职创立 Siebel Systems。埃里森暴怒。两人都是芝加哥出身、不愿认输的狠角色。
cases.trigger: Siebel's departure triggered a personal feud that consumed both executives through the second half of the 1990s.
cases.trigger_zh: 西贝尔的离开引发私人恩怨，贯穿 90 年代后半段。
cases.action: Ellison sent truckloads of ice cream sandwiches to Siebel's HQ with wrappers reading 'Summer is near. Oracle is here. To brighten your day and your career.' Meanwhile, Informix put up billboards near Oracle's Redwood Shores HQ: 'CAUTION: DINOSAUR CROSSING.' Informix CEO Phil White made it personal with a billboard showing Oracle's logo with a broken samurai sword — a jab at Ellison's love of Japanese culture.
cases.action_zh: 埃里森派一车车冰淇淋三明治到 Siebel 总部，包装上写着「夏天将至，甲骨文在此，照亮你的日子和事业。」Informix 则在甲骨文红木海岸总部附近立广告牌：「注意：恐龙出没。」Informix CEO 菲尔·怀特更私人化：广告牌上甲骨文 logo 配一把断掉的武士刀——讽刺埃里森对日本文化的热爱。
cases.result: While White was busy creating billboards, Informix imploded in a massive accounting scandal. White ended up in federal prison for securities fraud.
cases.result_zh: 怀特忙着做广告牌时，Informix 在重大会计丑闻中崩塌。怀特因证券欺诈入狱。
cases.after_state: Thiel's lesson: if you can't beat a rival, it may be better to merge. Which is exactly what happened next with PayPal and X.com.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 的教训：打不赢对手，不如合并。PayPal 与 X.com 正是如此。
cases.source_excerpt: Ellison sent truckloads of ice cream sandwiches to Siebel's headquarters to try to convince Siebel employees to jump ship. The copy on the wrappers? 'Summer is near. Oracle is here. To brighten your day and your career.'
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 埃里森派一车车冰淇淋三明治到 Siebel 总部，试图说服员工跳槽。包装上写着：「夏天将至，甲骨文在此，照亮你的日子和事业。」

cases.title: PayPal and X.com merge to stop destroying each other
cases.title_zh: PayPal 与 X.com 合并以停止互相消耗
cases.before_state: Confinity (PayPal) and Elon Musk's X.com had offices four blocks apart on University Avenue. Their products were feature-for-feature identical. Both teams were logging 100-hour weeks focused on destroying each other.
cases.before_state_zh: Confinity（PayPal）与马斯克的 X.com 在 University 大道相距四个街区。产品功能一模一样，两队每周工作 100 小时，一心要干掉对方。
cases.trigger: In February 2000, Elon and Thiel were more scared about the inflating tech bubble than about each other. A crash would ruin both before they could finish their fight.
cases.trigger_zh: 2000 年 2 月，埃隆和 Thiel 更怕科技泡沫膨胀，而非彼此。崩盘会在他们打完架之前毁掉两家。
cases.action: In early March, they met on neutral ground — a cafe almost exactly equidistant to their offices — and negotiated a 50-50 merger. One PayPal engineer had actually designed a bomb for the purpose of attacking X.com; calmer heads prevailed at a team meeting.
cases.action_zh: 3 月初，他们在中立地——一家几乎等距两家办公室的咖啡馆——谈成 50-50 合并。一位 PayPal 工程师曾设计过用于攻击 X.com 的炸弹；团队会议上理性占了上风。
cases.result: De-escalating the rivalry post-merger wasn't easy. But as a unified team, they rode out the dot-com crash and built a successful business.
cases.result_zh: 合并后化解敌意并不容易。但作为统一团队，他们熬过互联网泡沫崩盘，建立了成功业务。
cases.after_state: The merged company became PayPal, went public, and was acquired by eBay for $1.5 billion. The team became the PayPal Mafia.
cases.after_state_zh: 合并后的公司成为 PayPal，上市后被 eBay 以 15 亿美元收购。团队成为「PayPal 黑帮」。
hard_numbers: 4 blocks — distance between PayPal and X.com offices
100 hours — weekly work hours during the rivalry
hard_numbers_zh: 4 个街区 — PayPal 与 X.com 办公室距离
100 小时 — 竞争期间每周工时
50-50 — 合并条款
cases.source_excerpt: In early March we met on neutral ground—a café almost exactly equidistant to our offices—and negotiated a 50-50 merger. De-escalating the rivalry post-merger wasn't easy, but as far as problems go, it was a good one to have.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 3 月初我们在中立地——一家几乎等距两家办公室的咖啡馆——谈成 50-50 合并。合并后化解敌意不易，但算是不错的问题。

cases.title: 75% of PayPal's value came from profits beyond 2011
cases.title_zh: PayPal 75% 的价值来自 2011 年之后的利润
cases.before_state: PayPal had been in business for only 27 months. It hadn't made a profit yet. Revenue was growing 100% year-over-year, but conventional wisdom said unprofitable internet companies were worthless.
cases.before_state_zh: PayPal 成立仅 27 个月，尚未盈利。收入年增 100%，但传统观点认为不赚钱的互联网公司一文不值。
cases.trigger: Thiel projected PayPal's future cash flows and discovered something counterintuitive about the company's valuation.
cases.trigger_zh: Thiel 预测 PayPal 未来现金流，发现反直觉的估值结论。
cases.action: The analysis showed that 75% of PayPal's present value would come from profits generated in 2011 and beyond — a full decade away. Even that turned out to be an underestimation.
cases.action_zh: 分析显示 PayPal 现值的 75% 将来自 2011 年及以后的利润——整整十年之后。后来证明这还是低估。
cases.result: Today PayPal continues to grow at about 15% annually. Most of the company's value now appears to come from 2020 and beyond.
cases.result_zh: 如今 PayPal 年增约 15%。公司大部分价值似乎来自 2020 年及以后。
cases.after_state: This framework — that most of a tech company's value lies far in the future — became central to how Silicon Valley values startups. Short-term profitability is misleading.
cases.after_state_zh: 这一框架——科技公司大部分价值在遥远的未来——成为硅谷估值创业公司的核心。短期盈利会误导。
hard_numbers: 75% — share of value from profits after 2011
27 months — PayPal's age at time of analysis
15% — PayPal's annual growth rate at time of writing
hard_numbers_zh: 75% — 来自 2011 年后利润的价值占比
100% — 收入同比增长
27 个月 — 分析时 PayPal 成立时长
15% — 写作时 PayPal 年增长率
cases.source_excerpt: In March 2001, PayPal had yet to make a profit but our revenues were growing 100% year-over-year. When I projected our future cash flows, I found that 75% of the company's present value would come from profits generated in 2011 and beyond—hard to believe for a company that had been in business for only 27 months.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 2001 年 3 月 PayPal 尚未盈利，但收入年增 100%。预测未来现金流时，我发现公司现值的 75% 将来自 2011 年及以后——对一家成立仅 27 个月的公司难以置信。

cases.title: Facebook starts by monopolizing just Harvard, then sequences outward
cases.title_zh: Facebook 先垄断哈佛，再向外扩展
cases.before_state: Friendster and MySpace targeted everyone. Xanadu had tried and failed to build a universal network in the 1990s after three decades of effort — it could only work at scale, so it never worked at all.
cases.before_state_zh: Friendster 和 MySpace 面向所有人。Xanadu 三十年努力后在 90 年代尝试建立通用网络失败——只能大规模运作，所以从未成功。
cases.trigger: Zuckerberg took the opposite approach: build for the smallest possible market first.
cases.trigger_zh: 扎克伯格反其道而行：先为最小市场打造。
cases.action: Facebook's first product was designed to get every single Harvard student signed up — not to attract all people on Earth. Only after dominating Harvard did it expand to other Ivy League schools, then all colleges, then high schools, then everyone.
cases.action_zh: Facebook 首款产品旨在让每个哈佛学生注册——而非吸引全人类。垄断哈佛后才扩展到其他常春藤、所有大学、高中，最后所有人。
cases.result: Near-total penetration at each stage created genuine network effects that Friendster and MySpace never achieved. Successful network businesses rarely get started by MBA types because the initial markets are so small they don't even appear to be business opportunities.
cases.result_zh: 每阶段近乎全覆盖创造了 Friendster 和 MySpace 从未实现的真正网络效应。成功的网络业务很少由 MBA 型人创立，因为初始市场小到不像商机。
cases.after_state: Facebook's deliberate sequencing became Thiel's go-to example of how to build a monopoly. Start tiny, dominate, expand.
cases.after_state_zh: Facebook 的刻意递进成为 Thiel 打造垄断的经典案例：从小做起、垄断、扩张。

quotes.text: The perfect target market for a startup is a small group of particular people concentrated together and served by few or no competitors.
quotes.text_zh: 创业公司最理想的目标市场是一小群集中、几乎没有竞争对手服务的人。
quotes.speaker: Peter Thiel

cases.source_excerpt: Facebook started with just Harvard students—Mark Zuckerberg's first product was designed to get all his classmates signed up, not to attract all people of Earth. This is why successful network businesses rarely get started by MBA types: the initial markets are so small that they often don't even appear to be business opportunities at all.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: Facebook 从哈佛学生起步——扎克伯格首款产品旨在让全班同学注册，而非吸引全人类。因此成功的网络业务很少由 MBA 型人创立：初始市场小到不像商机。

cases.title: Amazon sequences from books to everything
cases.title_zh: 亚马逊从图书递进到万物
cases.before_state: Bezos's founding vision was to dominate all of online retail. But he deliberately started with just one category.
cases.before_state_zh: 贝佐斯创始愿景是主导在线零售，但刻意从单一品类起步。
cases.trigger: Books were the ideal starting niche: millions to catalog, all the same shape, easy to ship. The rarest titles drew the most enthusiastic customers — people located far from bookstores.
cases.trigger_zh: 图书是理想起点：数百万册可编目、形状统一、易配送。最稀有的书吸引最热情的买家——远离书店的人。
cases.action: Amazon first monopolized online book sales, claiming to be 'Earth's largest bookstore' without physically stocking inventory. Barnes & Noble sued, claiming Amazon was a 'book broker' not a 'bookstore.' Then Amazon expanded to CDs, videos, software — and gradually became the world's general store.
cases.action_zh: 亚马逊先垄断在线图书销售，自称「全球最大书店」却无实体库存。巴诺起诉称亚马逊是「图书经纪」而非「书店」。随后亚马逊扩展到 CD、视频、软件——逐渐成为全球百货。
cases.result: The name 'Amazon' brilliantly encapsulated the scaling strategy — from the biodiversity of the rain forest (all books) to every kind of thing in the world.
cases.result_zh: 「亚马逊」这个名字巧妙概括了扩张策略——从雨林生物多样性（所有书）到世间万物。
cases.after_state: eBay, which also started in niches (Beanie Babies), showed the limits of this approach: auctions worked for unique items but not commodities. People don't want to bid on pencils.
cases.after_state_zh: eBay 也从 niche（豆豆娃）起步，但展示了局限：拍卖适合独特品而非日用品。没人想为铅笔竞价。
cases.source_excerpt: Jeff Bezos's founding vision was to dominate all of online retail, but he very deliberately started with books... Amazon then had two options: expand the number of people who read books, or expand to adjacent markets. They chose the latter.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 贝佐斯创始愿景是主导在线零售，但刻意从图书起步……亚马逊之后有两个选择：扩大读书人数，或扩张到相邻市场。他们选了后者。

cases.title: Pets.com burns $300M because nobody asked if online pet supplies was worth fighting for
cases.title_zh: Pets.com 烧掉 3 亿美元：没人问在线宠物用品值不值得争
cases.before_state: During dot-com mania, multiple startups raced to dominate online pet supplies — a market whose size and viability nobody had validated.
cases.before_state_zh: 互联网泡沫期间，多家创业公司争抢在线宠物用品市场——规模与可行性无人验证。
cases.trigger: Competition made companies hallucinate opportunities. Each was obsessed with defeating its rivals on tactical questions like pricing chewy dog toys and creating Super Bowl ads.
cases.trigger_zh: 竞争让公司产生机会幻觉。每家都沉迷于在战术上击败对手：狗咬胶定价、超级碗广告。
cases.action: Pets.com, PetStore.com, Petopia.com, and what seemed like dozens of others competed ferociously. They lost sight of the wider question: was this even the right market to be in?
cases.action_zh: Pets.com、PetStore.com、Petopia.com 及数十家竞品激烈厮杀。他们忽视了更根本的问题：这是否是正确的市场？
cases.result: When Pets.com folded after the crash, $300 million of investment capital vanished. The others followed.
cases.result_zh: Pets.com 崩盘后 3 亿美元投资蒸发。其他公司紧随其后。
cases.after_state: Thiel's lesson: winning is better than losing, but everybody loses when the war isn't one worth fighting.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 的教训：赢比输好，但若战争不值得打，人人皆输。
hard_numbers: $300 million — investment capital lost with Pets.com
hard_numbers_zh: 3 亿美元 — Pets.com 烧掉的投资

quotes.text: Competition can make people hallucinate opportunities where none exist.
quotes.text_zh: 竞争会让人在不存在机会的地方产生幻觉。
quotes.speaker: Peter Thiel

cases.source_excerpt: Amid all the tactical questions—Who could price chewy dog toys most aggressively? Who could create the best Super Bowl ads?—these companies totally lost sight of the wider question of whether the online pet supply market was the right space to be in.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 在战术问题中——谁能把狗咬胶定得更狠？谁能做出最好的超级碗广告？——这些公司完全忽视了更根本的问题：在线宠物用品是否是正确的赛道。

cases.title: Thiel's best failure: the Supreme Court clerkship he didn't get
cases.title_zh: Thiel 最好的失败：没拿到最高法院书记员
cases.before_state: Thiel was a top Stanford Law student. He competed obsessively for the highest prize: a Supreme Court clerkship. Only a few dozen graduates per year out of tens of thousands got one.
cases.before_state_zh: Thiel 是斯坦福法学院尖子生，痴迷竞争最高奖：最高法院书记员。每年数万毕业生中仅几十人获得。
cases.trigger: After interviews with Justices Kennedy and Scalia that went well, Thiel was devastated when he didn't get the clerkship. He thought it was the end.
cases.trigger_zh: 与肯尼迪、斯卡利亚大法官面试顺利后，Thiel 未获聘，深受打击。他以为人生完了。
cases.action: Years later, after building and selling PayPal, Thiel ran into an old law school friend. The friend's first question wasn't 'How are you?' It was: 'So, Peter, aren't you glad you didn't get that clerkship?'
cases.action_zh: 多年后，创立并出售 PayPal 后，Thiel 偶遇法学院老友。对方第一句不是「你好吗」，而是：「彼得，你没拿到那个书记员，是不是挺庆幸的？」
cases.result: Had he clerked on the Supreme Court, Thiel probably would have spent his entire career taking depositions or drafting other people's business deals instead of creating anything new.
cases.result_zh: 若真去了最高法院，Thiel 很可能一辈子做取证、起草别人的商业合同，而非创造新事物。
cases.after_state: He uses this as his core example of the competition trap: so focused on winning the tournament that you never ask whether the prize is worth having.
cases.after_state_zh: 他用此例说明竞争陷阱：太专注赢得锦标赛，从不问奖品是否值得。

quotes.text: So, Peter, aren't you glad you didn't get that clerkship?
quotes.text_zh: 彼得，你没拿到那个书记员，是不是挺庆幸的？
quotes.speaker: unnamed law school friend

cases.source_excerpt: If only I got the clerkship, I thought, I would be set for life. But I didn't... Had I actually clerked on the Supreme Court, I probably would have spent my entire career taking depositions or drafting other people's business deals instead of creating anything new.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 我想，只要拿到书记员，人生就稳了。但我没拿到……若真去了最高法院，我可能一辈子做取证或起草别人的商业合同，而非创造新事物。

cases.title: Zuckerberg rejects Yahoo's $1 billion offer in 10 minutes
cases.title_zh: 扎克伯格 10 分钟拒绝雅虎 10 亿美元报价
cases.before_state: Yahoo! offered to buy Facebook for $1 billion. The board, including Thiel, thought they should at least consider it.
cases.before_state_zh: 雅虎出价 10 亿美元收购 Facebook。包括 Thiel 在内的董事会认为至少该考虑。
cases.trigger: Zuckerberg walked into the board meeting with his mind already made up.
cases.trigger_zh: 扎克伯格走进董事会时心意已决。
cases.action: Zuckerberg announced: 'Okay, guys, this is just a formality, it shouldn't take more than 10 minutes. We're obviously not going to sell here.' Mark saw where he could take the company. Yahoo! didn't.
cases.action_zh: 扎克伯格宣布：「各位，这只是走形式，不超过 10 分钟。我们显然不会卖。」马克看到了公司能走多远，雅虎没有。
cases.result: Facebook went on to become one of the most valuable companies in history. The $1 billion offer looks absurd in retrospect.
cases.result_zh: Facebook 后来成为史上最有价值公司之一。10 亿美元报价如今看来荒谬。
cases.after_state: Thiel's lesson: a business with a good definite plan will always be underrated in a world where people see the future as random. Definite founders with robust plans don't sell.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 的教训：在人们把未来视为随机时，有明确计划的公司总被低估。有坚定计划的创始人不会卖。
hard_numbers: 10 minutes — duration of the board meeting
hard_numbers_zh: 10 亿美元 — 雅虎对 Facebook 的报价
10 分钟 — 董事会会议时长

quotes.text: Okay, guys, this is just a formality, it shouldn't take more than 10 minutes. We're obviously not going to sell here.
quotes.text_zh: 各位，这只是走形式，不超过 10 分钟。我们显然不会卖。
quotes.speaker: Mark Zuckerberg

cases.source_excerpt: When Yahoo! offered to buy Facebook for $1 billion in July 2006, I thought we should at least consider it. But Mark Zuckerberg walked into the board meeting and announced: 'Okay, guys, this is just a formality, it shouldn't take more than 10 minutes. We're obviously not going to sell here.'
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 2006 年 7 月雅虎出价 10 亿收购 Facebook 时，我觉得至少该考虑。但扎克伯格走进董事会宣布：「各位，这只是走形式，不超过 10 分钟。我们显然不会卖。」

cases.title: Facebook returns more than all other Founders Fund investments combined
cases.title_zh: Facebook 回报超过 Founders Fund 其他投资总和
cases.before_state: Most VCs assumed venture returns follow a normal distribution: bad companies fail, mediocre ones stay flat, good ones return 2-4x. This 'spray and pray' approach led to diversified portfolios.
cases.before_state_zh: 多数 VC 假设风投回报呈正态分布：差公司倒闭、平庸持平、好公司 2-4 倍。这种「撒网祈祷」导致分散投资。
cases.trigger: Thiel analyzed Founders Fund's 2005 fund returns and discovered an extreme power law pattern.
cases.trigger_zh: Thiel 分析 Founders Fund 2005 基金回报，发现极端幂律分布。
cases.action: Facebook, the best investment, returned more than all other investments in the fund combined. Palantir, the second-best, returned more than everything except Facebook. This pattern repeated across all their funds.
cases.action_zh: Facebook 作为最佳投资，回报超过基金其他投资总和。Palantir 第二，回报超过除 Facebook 外所有投资。这一模式在所有基金中重复。
cases.result: The biggest secret in venture capital: the best investment in a successful fund equals or outperforms the entire rest of the fund combined.
cases.result_zh: 风投最大秘密：成功基金中最佳投资等于或超过其余投资总和。
cases.after_state: This led to two rules: (1) only invest in companies that could return the whole fund, and (2) since that eliminates most investments, there can't be any other rules. Andreessen Horowitz's $250K Instagram investment returned $78M — a 312x return — but wasn't enough for a $1.5 billion fund.
cases.after_state_zh: 由此得出两条规则：(1) 只投能回报整个基金的公司；(2) 既然这排除了大多数，就不能有其他规则。a16z 25 万美元投 Instagram 回报 7800 万——312 倍——但对 15 亿基金不够。
hard_numbers: 19 — number of Instagrams needed to break even on $250K checks
hard_numbers_zh: 25 万美元 — a16z 对 Instagram 投资
7800 万美元 — a16z Instagram 回报
312 倍 — Instagram 回报倍数
15 亿美元 — a16z 基金规模
19 — 25 万支票回本需要的 Instagram 数量
cases.source_excerpt: Facebook, the best investment in our 2005 fund, returned more than all the others combined. Palantir, the second-best investment, is set to return more than the sum of every other investment aside from Facebook.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 我们 2005 基金的最佳投资 Facebook，回报超过其他所有投资总和。第二的 Palantir，回报将超过除 Facebook 外所有投资之和。

cases.title: Hewlett-Packard stops inventing and loses $112 billion in value
cases.title_zh: 惠普停止创新，损失 1120 亿美元市值
cases.before_state: In 1990, HP was worth $9 billion. Through the '90s, it released a stream of pioneering products: the first affordable color printer (1991), one of the first superportable laptops (1993), the first all-in-one printer/fax/copier (1994). By mid-2000, HP was worth $135 billion.
cases.before_state_zh: 1990 年惠普价值 90 亿。90 年代推出一系列开创性产品：首款平价彩打(1991)、首批超便携笔记本(1993)、首款一体机(1994)。2000 年中市值 1350 亿。
cases.trigger: Starting in late 1999, HP launched a branding campaign around 'invent' but stopped actually inventing. In 2002, it merged with Compaq — a sign it didn't know what else to do.
cases.trigger_zh: 1999 年末起，惠普围绕「invent」做品牌，却停止真正创新。2002 年并购康柏——不知还能做什么。
cases.action: The board split into two factions. Tom Perkins (73, an engineer hired by Bill Hewlett himself) wanted to identify and build promising new technologies. Patricia Dunn (a banker) argued the board should just play night watchman — checking accounting and rules, not charting technology direction. Dunn's faction won.
cases.action_zh: 董事会分裂。汤姆·珀金斯(73 岁，比尔·休利特亲自招的工程师)想识别并打造新技术。帕特里夏·邓恩(银行家)主张董事会只当守夜人——查账和合规，不规划技术方向。邓恩派胜出。
cases.result: By 2005, HP's market cap had plunged to $70 billion. Then Dunn arranged illegal wiretaps to find a board leaker, causing a scandal worse than the original dysfunction. By late 2012, HP was worth just $23 billion.
cases.result_zh: 2005 年惠普市值跌至 700 亿。邓恩安排非法窃听查董事会泄密者，丑闻比原内部混乱更糟。2012 年末惠普仅值 230 亿。
cases.after_state: HP went from $135 billion to $23 billion — not because of competition but because it stopped believing in secrets. Having abandoned the search for technological secrets, HP obsessed over gossip.
cases.after_state_zh: 惠普从 1350 亿跌至 230 亿——非因竞争，而是不再相信秘密。放弃技术秘密后，惠普沉迷八卦。
hard_numbers_zh: 90 亿 — 1990 年惠普市值
concepts_zh: 工程 vs 治理思维
cases.source_excerpt: But starting in late 1999, when HP introduced a new branding campaign around the imperative to 'invent,' it stopped inventing things... Having abandoned the search for technological secrets, HP obsessed over gossip.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 但 1999 年末起，惠普推出「invent」品牌活动后停止真正创新……放弃技术秘密后，惠普沉迷八卦。

cases.title: Luke Nosek's first startup fails because co-founders were strangers
cases.title_zh: Luke Nosek 首次创业失败：联合创始人是陌生人
cases.before_state: Luke Nosek, later a PayPal co-founder, started his first company with someone he met at a networking event. They talked briefly and decided to start a company together.
cases.before_state_zh: 后来的 PayPal 联合创始人 Luke Nosek 与在社交活动上认识的人一起创业。他们简短交谈后决定合伙。
cases.trigger: Luke was a brilliant, eccentric thinker. His co-founder was an MBA type who didn't want to miss the '90s gold rush. Their visions were fundamentally incompatible.
cases.trigger_zh: Luke 是才华横溢的怪才。联合创始人是 MBA 型，不想错过 90 年代淘金热。两人愿景根本不合。
cases.action: Their company blew up. Thiel, who had invested in it, lost his money.
cases.action_zh: 公司崩了。投资的 Thiel 亏了钱。
cases.result: Thiel formulated his principle: choosing a co-founder is like getting married. Meeting someone at a networking event and starting a company is like marrying the first person you meet at a slot machine in Vegas.
cases.result_zh: Thiel 总结原则：选联合创始人像结婚。在社交活动认识人然后创业，像在拉斯维加斯老虎机旁遇到第一个人就结婚。
cases.after_state: After this failure, Thiel and Nosek co-founded PayPal together — this time with people they actually knew. They still work together at Founders Fund today.
cases.after_state_zh: 这次失败后，Thiel 和 Nosek 一起创立 PayPal——这次是和真正认识的人。他们至今仍在 Founders Fund 共事。
cases.source_excerpt: They met at a networking event, talked for a while, and decided to start a company together. That's no better than marrying the first person you meet at the slot machines in Vegas: you might hit the jackpot, but it probably won't work.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 他们在社交活动认识，聊了一会儿就决定合伙创业。这和在拉斯维加斯老虎机旁遇到第一个人就结婚差不多：可能中奖，但多半不行。

cases.title: Box CEO sleeps on a mattress two blocks from HQ for four years
cases.title_zh: Box CEO 四年睡在总部两街区外的床垫上
cases.before_state: Aaron Levie had co-founded Box, a cloud storage company. The challenge was keeping a startup culture of commitment while growing.
cases.before_state_zh: Aaron Levie 联合创立了云存储公司 Box。挑战是在增长中保持创业公司的投入文化。
cases.trigger: Thiel noticed that CEO salary is one of the clearest predictors of startup success — the less the CEO pays himself, the better the company does.
cases.trigger_zh: Thiel 发现 CEO 薪资是创业成功最清晰的预测指标之一——CEO 拿得越少，公司越好。
cases.action: Levie was always careful to pay himself less than everyone else in the company. Four years after founding Box, he was still living two blocks from HQ in a one-bedroom apartment with no furniture except a mattress.
cases.action_zh: Levie 始终确保自己拿得比公司任何人都少。创立 Box 四年后，他仍住在总部两街区外的一居室，除床垫外无家具。
cases.result: Every employee noticed his commitment and emulated it. The culture of sacrifice became self-reinforcing.
cases.result_zh: 每位员工都注意到他的投入并效仿。牺牲文化自我强化。
cases.after_state: Thiel's rule: a CEO of an early-stage startup should never receive more than $150,000/year. If a CEO collects $300,000, he becomes more like a politician defending the status quo than a founder fixing problems aggressively.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 的规则：早期创业公司 CEO 年薪不应超过 15 万。若拿 30 万，他更像捍卫现状的政客，而非积极解决问题的创始人。
hard_numbers: $150,000 — Thiel's maximum CEO salary for early-stage startups
4 years — time Levie lived on a mattress near HQ
hard_numbers_zh: 15 万美元 — Thiel 对早期创业 CEO 的最高年薪
4 年 — Levie 在总部附近睡床垫的时长
concepts_zh: 现金 vs 股权心态
cases.source_excerpt: Aaron Levie, the CEO of Box, was always careful to pay himself less than everyone else in the company—four years after he started Box, he was still living two blocks away from HQ in a one-bedroom apartment with no furniture except a mattress.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: Box CEO Aaron Levie 始终确保自己拿得比公司任何人都少——创立 Box 四年后，他仍住在总部两街区外的一居室，除床垫外无家具。

cases.title: The PayPal Mafia: one team spawns seven billion-dollar companies
cases.title_zh: PayPal 黑帮：一支团队孵化七家十亿美元公司
cases.before_state: PayPal was sold to eBay for $1.5 billion in 2002. The team dispersed.
cases.before_state_zh: 2002 年 PayPal 以 15 亿美元卖给 eBay。团队散去。
cases.trigger: Rather than going their separate ways like typical corporate alumni, the PayPal team maintained tight bonds and helped each other start and invest in new companies.
cases.trigger_zh: 与典型企业校友各奔东西不同，PayPal 团队保持紧密联系，互相帮助创业和投资。
cases.action: Elon Musk founded SpaceX and co-founded Tesla. Reid Hoffman co-founded LinkedIn. Steve Chen, Chad Hurley, and Jawed Karim founded YouTube. Jeremy Stoppelman and Russel Simmons founded Yelp. David Sacks co-founded Yammer. Thiel co-founded Palantir.
cases.action_zh: 埃隆·马斯克创立 SpaceX、联合创立特斯拉。Reid Hoffman 联合创立 LinkedIn。Steve Chen、Chad Hurley、Jawed Karim 创立 YouTube。Jeremy Stoppelman、Russel Simmons 创立 Yelp。David Sacks 联合创立 Yammer。Thiel 联合创立 Palantir。
cases.result: All seven post-PayPal companies became worth more than $1 billion each.
cases.result_zh: 这七家 PayPal 后公司各自估值超 10 亿。
cases.after_state: Thiel's conclusion: the culture was strong enough to transcend the original company. This is what happens when you hire people who genuinely like each other, not just credentialed strangers. PayPal's office amenities never got press, but the team's outcomes were extraordinary.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 的结论：文化强到超越原公司。当你招的是真正互相喜欢的人而非有学历的陌生人，就会如此。PayPal 的办公室福利从未上头条，但团队成果非凡。
hard_numbers_zh: 15 亿美元 — eBay 收购 PayPal
7 — 孵化的十亿美元公司数量
cases.source_excerpt: We sold PayPal to eBay for $1.5 billion in 2002. Since then, Elon Musk has founded SpaceX and co-founded Tesla Motors; Reid Hoffman co-founded LinkedIn; Steve Chen, Chad Hurley, and Jawed Karim together founded YouTube... Today all seven of those companies are worth more than $1 billion each.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 2002 年我们把 PayPal 以 15 亿卖给 eBay。此后马斯克创立 SpaceX、联合创立特斯拉；Hoffman 联合创立 LinkedIn；Chen、Hurley、Karim 创立 YouTube……如今这七家公司各自估值超 10 亿。

cases.title: PayPal hires Star Trek's Scotty as spokesman — and it flops
cases.title_zh: PayPal 请《星际迷航》斯科蒂代言——惨败
cases.before_state: PayPal needed publicity for its PalmPilot payment product. The team were all nerds who thought engineering authority mattered more than star power.
cases.before_state_zh: PayPal 需要为 PalmPilot 支付产品造势。团队都是书呆子，认为工程权威比明星效应重要。
cases.trigger: They hired James Doohan (Scotty from Star Trek) instead of William Shatner (Captain Kirk), reasoning that the Chief Engineer had more credibility than a 'showboating' captain.
cases.trigger_zh: 他们请了詹姆斯·杜汉（《星际迷航》斯科蒂）而非威廉·夏特纳（柯克船长），理由是总工程师比「爱秀」的船长更有可信度。
cases.action: At the launch event, Scotty recited: 'I've been beaming people up my whole career, but this is the first time I've ever been able to beam money!' The few journalists who came weren't impressed.
cases.action_zh: 发布会上斯科蒂念道：「我职业生涯一直在传送人，但这是我第一次能传送钱！」来的少数记者并不买账。
cases.result: It flopped completely. Meanwhile, Priceline.com later cast Shatner in a famous series of TV spots — and it worked. But Priceline was already a major company by then.
cases.result_zh: 完全惨败。Priceline.com 后来请夏特纳拍了一系列著名电视广告——成功了。但那时 Priceline 已是大公司。
cases.after_state: Thiel's lesson: no early-stage startup can compete with big companies' advertising budgets. Captain Kirk is in a league of his own. Startups need viral distribution, not celebrity endorsements.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 的教训：早期创业公司无法与大公司广告预算竞争。柯克船长独一档。创业公司需要病毒式传播，而非明星代言。

quotes.text: I've been beaming people up my whole career, but this is the first time I've ever been able to beam money!
quotes.text_zh: 我职业生涯一直在传送人，但这是我第一次能传送钱！
quotes.speaker: James Doohan (as spokesman)

cases.source_excerpt: At PayPal we hired James Doohan, who played Scotty on Star Trek, to be our official spokesman... It flopped—the few who actually came to cover the event weren't impressed. We were all nerds, so we had thought Scotty the Chief Engineer could speak with more authority than, say, Captain Kirk.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: PayPal 请了《星际迷航》中演斯科蒂的詹姆斯·杜汉当官方代言人……惨败——来报道的少数记者并不买账。我们都是书呆子，以为总工程师斯科蒂比柯克船长更有权威。

cases.title: SpaceX wins billion-dollar NASA contracts through complex sales mastery
cases.title_zh: SpaceX 凭复杂销售拿下 NASA 十亿美元合同
cases.before_state: The traditional U.S. aerospace industry employed over 500,000 people spread across all 50 states. Members of Congress didn't want to give up federal funds going to their districts.
cases.before_state_zh: 传统美国航天业雇佣 50 州超 50 万人。国会议员不愿放弃流向本选区的联邦资金。
cases.trigger: SpaceX, with just 3,000 employees mostly in California, needed to win NASA contracts to survive. This required not just better rockets but political mastery.
cases.trigger_zh: SpaceX 仅 3000 多员工、多在加州，需拿下 NASA 合同才能生存。这需要的不只是更好的火箭，还有政治手腕。
cases.action: Elon Musk personally handled the complex sales. Because these deals happen only a few times per year, a sales grandmaster can focus on the most crucial people — and even overcome political inertia. There were no separate 'salespeople' — the CEO was the sales force.
cases.action_zh: 埃隆·马斯克亲自操盘复杂销售。因这类交易每年仅几次，销售大师可聚焦最关键的人——甚至克服政治惯性。没有单独的「销售」——CEO 就是销售团队。
cases.result: Musk persuaded NASA to sign billion-dollar contracts to replace the decommissioned space shuttle with SpaceX vessels.
cases.result_zh: 马斯克说服 NASA 签下十亿美元合同，用 SpaceX 飞船替代退役航天飞机。
cases.after_state: Similarly, Palantir CEO Alex Karp spends 25 days a month on the road meeting clients. At deal sizes of $1-100 million, buyers want to talk to the CEO, not the VP of Sales.
cases.after_state_zh: 同理，Palantir CEO Alex Karp 每月 25 天在路上见客户。1-100 亿美元的单子，买家想和 CEO 谈，不是销售副总。
hard_numbers: 25 days/month — Alex Karp's time on the road
hard_numbers_zh: 3000+ — SpaceX 员工数
50 万+ — 传统航天业 50 州工人
100 万-1 亿美元 — Palantir 单笔交易规模
25 天/月 — Karp 出差见客户时间
cases.source_excerpt: Within just a few years of launching his rocket startup, Elon Musk persuaded NASA to sign billion-dollar contracts to replace the decommissioned space shuttle with a newly designed vessel from SpaceX.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 火箭创业公司成立仅几年，埃隆·马斯克就说服 NASA 签下十亿美元合同，用 SpaceX 新设计的飞船替代退役航天飞机。

cases.title: Box sells Stanford Sleep Clinic first, then captures the whole university
cases.title_zh: Box 先卖给斯坦福睡眠诊所，再拿下整所大学
cases.before_state: Box had a good cloud storage product but people didn't know they needed it — cloud computing hadn't caught on yet. Blake was hired as Box's third salesperson.
cases.before_state_zh: Box 有好的云存储产品，但人们不知道自己需要——云计算尚未普及。Blake 被聘为 Box 第三位销售。
cases.trigger: Instead of trying enterprise-wide sales to university presidents, Box started with small groups of users who had the most acute file-sharing problems.
cases.trigger_zh: Box 没有先找校长做企业级销售，而是从文件共享痛点最突出的小群体入手。
cases.action: In 2009, Blake sold a small Box account to the Stanford Sleep Clinic, where researchers needed an easy, secure way to store data logs. From that tiny foothold, Box built relationships with more users across the university.
cases.action_zh: 2009 年 Blake 把小型 Box 账户卖给斯坦福睡眠诊所，研究人员需要安全便捷存储实验数据。从这一小据点，Box 与校内更多用户建立关系。
cases.result: Today Stanford offers a Stanford-branded Box account to every student and faculty member. Stanford Hospital runs on Box.
cases.result_zh: 如今斯坦福为每位师生提供斯坦福品牌 Box 账户。斯坦福医院运行在 Box 上。
cases.after_state: If Box had tried a complex enterprise sale to the university president first, it would have sold nothing and become a forgotten startup. Personal sales to small groups made it a multibillion-dollar business.
cases.after_state_zh: 若 Box 先找校长做复杂企业销售，会一无所获、沦为被遗忘的创业公司。对小群体的个人销售让它成为数十亿美元业务。
cases.source_excerpt: In 2009, Blake sold a small Box account to the Stanford Sleep Clinic, where researchers needed an easy, secure way to store experimental data logs. Today the university offers a Stanford-branded Box account to every one of its students and faculty members.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 2009 年 Blake 把小型 Box 账户卖给斯坦福睡眠诊所，研究人员需要安全便捷存储实验数据。如今斯坦福为每位师生提供斯坦福品牌 Box 账户。

cases.title: PayPal's fraud crisis births the human-machine hybrid that became Palantir
cases.title_zh: PayPal 欺诈危机催生人机混合，演变为 Palantir
cases.before_state: PayPal was losing over $10 million per month to credit card fraud. They were processing hundreds of transactions per minute — no human team could review them all. But pure software couldn't keep up either: fraudsters adapted faster than the algorithms.
cases.before_state_zh: PayPal 每月因信用卡欺诈损失超 1000 万美元。每分钟处理数百笔交易——人力无法逐一审核。纯软件也跟不上：欺诈者比算法适应更快。
cases.trigger: Max Levchin's elite math team wrote software to auto-detect fraud, but the thieves would catch on within an hour or two and change tactics. The adaptive enemy defeated pure automation.
cases.trigger_zh: Max Levchin 的精英数学团队写了自动检测欺诈的软件，但窃贼一两个小时就会察觉并改变策略。适应性敌人击败了纯自动化。
cases.action: They invented a hybrid approach called 'Igor' (named after a Russian fraudster who bragged they'd never stop him): software flagged the most suspicious transactions, and human operators made the final judgment. Neither humans nor machines alone could have solved it.
cases.action_zh: 他们发明了名为「Igor」的混合方案（以一位吹嘘没人能阻止他的俄罗斯欺诈者命名）：软件标记最可疑交易，人工做最终判断。人机缺一不可。
cases.result: PayPal turned its first quarterly profit in Q1 2002 (vs. a $29.3 million quarterly loss one year before). The FBI asked to use Igor for detecting financial crime.
cases.result_zh: PayPal 在 2002 年 Q1 首次季度盈利（一年前季度亏损 2930 万）。FBI 请求用 Igor 检测金融犯罪。
cases.after_state: In 2004, Thiel co-founded Palantir with Alex Karp and Stephen Cohen, applying the same human-computer hybrid principle to counterterrorism and intelligence. Palantir was on track to book $1 billion in sales by 2014.
cases.after_state_zh: 2004 年 Thiel 与 Alex Karp、Stephen Cohen 联合创立 Palantir，将同一人机混合原则应用于反恐与情报。Palantir 2014 年销售额预计达 10 亿。
hard_numbers_zh: 1000 万美元/月 — PayPal 欺诈损失峰值
2930 万美元 — Igor 前季度亏损
2002 Q1 — 首次季度盈利
2004 — Palantir 成立
10 亿美元 — Palantir 2014 年预计销售额
cases.source_excerpt: We were losing upwards of $10 million to credit card fraud every month... the thieves would catch on and change their tactics... So Max and his engineers rewrote the software to take a hybrid approach: the computer would flag the most suspicious transactions, and human operators would make the final judgment.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 我们每月因信用卡欺诈损失超 1000 万……窃贼会察觉并改变策略……Max 和工程师重写软件，采用混合方案：计算机标记最可疑交易，人工做最终判断。

cases.title: Solyndra and 40+ solar companies fail all seven questions
cases.title_zh: Solyndra 及 40 多家太阳能公司七问全败
cases.before_state: In 2006, VC John Doerr declared 'green is the new red, white and blue.' Over $50 billion poured into cleantech. Everyone agreed the next big thing was clean energy.
cases.before_state_zh: 2006 年 VC 约翰·杜尔宣称「绿色是新红白蓝」。超 500 亿美元涌入清洁技术。人人认同下一件大事是清洁能源。
cases.trigger: Companies confused macro-level social need (the world needs clean energy) with micro-level business opportunity. They started companies with zero good answers to Thiel's seven questions.
cases.trigger_zh: 公司把宏观社会需求（世界需要清洁能源）与微观商业机会混淆。他们以对 Thiel 七问零好答案创业。
cases.action: Solyndra made cylindrical solar cells that were geometrically 1/pi as efficient as flat ones. MiaSole's CEO told Congress his company was one of 'several very strong' startups — then predicted they'd be the largest. Better Place raised $800 million to build battery-swap stations but sold only 1,000 cars because the buying process was absurdly complex. Cleantech executives wore suits — a red flag, because real technologists wear T-shirts.
cases.action_zh: Solyndra 做圆柱形太阳能电池，几何效率仅为平板 1/π。MiaSole CEO 告诉国会公司是「几家非常强」创业公司之一——又预测会成为最大。Better Place 融资 8 亿建换电站，但因购买流程荒谬复杂只卖了 1000 辆车。清洁技术高管穿西装——红旗，因为真正技术人穿 T 恤。
cases.result: Solyndra got $535 million in federal loans and went bankrupt in 2011. Over 40 solar manufacturers went bankrupt in 2012 alone. MiaSole was acquired for hundreds of millions less than its investors put in. Better Place sold its assets for $12 million.
cases.result_zh: Solyndra 获 5.35 亿联邦贷款，2011 年破产。2012 年超 40 家太阳能制造商破产。MiaSole 被收购价低于投资者投入数亿。Better Place 资产以 1200 万出售。
cases.after_state: Thiel codified the '7 Questions' framework from this failure: Technology, Timing, Monopoly, People, Distribution, Durability, Secret. Then showed how Tesla answered all seven correctly.
cases.after_state_zh: Thiel 从此次失败总结「七问」框架：技术、时机、垄断、人、渠道、持久、秘密。然后展示特斯拉如何七问全对。
hard_numbers_zh: 500+ 亿美元 — 清洁技术总投资
5.35 亿美元 — Solyndra 联邦贷款
40+ — 2012 年太阳能破产数
8 亿美元 — Better Place 融资
1000 — Better Place 售出车辆
1200 万美元 — Better Place 资产出售

quotes.text: Green is the new red, white and blue.
quotes.speaker: John Doerr

quotes.text: The technical challenges we overcame successfully, but the other obstacles we were not able to overcome.
quotes.text_zh: 技术挑战我们成功克服了，但其他障碍我们未能克服。
quotes.speaker: Better Place board

cases.source_excerpt: The striking thing about the cleantech bubble was that people were starting companies with zero good answers—and that meant hoping for a miracle... More than 40 solar manufacturers went out of business or filed for bankruptcy in 2012 alone.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 清洁技术泡沫的惊人之处是人们以零好答案创业——意味着指望奇迹……2012 年超 40 家太阳能制造商倒闭或破产。

cases.title: Tesla answers all seven questions while cleantech burns
cases.title_zh: 清洁技术烧成灰时，特斯拉七问全对
cases.before_state: The cleantech industry was collapsing. Dozens of solar companies had gone bankrupt. Investors were cynical about all green technology.
cases.before_state_zh: 清洁技术行业崩塌。数十家太阳能公司破产。投资者对所有绿色技术持怀疑态度。
cases.trigger: While every other cleantech company failed Thiel's seven questions, Tesla answered every single one correctly.
cases.trigger_zh: 其他清洁技术公司七问全败时，特斯拉七问全对。
cases.action: Technology: so good that Daimler, Mercedes, Toyota, and GM all relied on Tesla's components. Timing: secured a $465 million DOE loan in January 2010 — the only moment it was possible. Monopoly: started with high-end electric sports cars, a tiny niche. People: Musk was an engineer-CEO, not a suit. Distribution: owned its own stores. Durability: had a head start moving faster than anyone. Secret: 'green' could mean 'cool' — Leonardo DiCaprio ditched his Prius for a Tesla Roadster.
cases.action_zh: 技术：好到戴姆勒、奔驰、丰田、通用都依赖特斯拉零部件。时机：2010 年 1 月获 4.65 亿 DOE 贷款——唯一可能窗口。垄断：从高端电动跑车小 niche 起步。人：马斯克是工程师型 CEO，非西装。渠道：自有门店。持久：先发优势、行动最快。秘密：「绿色」可意味「酷」——莱昂纳多弃普锐斯换特斯拉 Roadster。
cases.result: Roadster: $109,000, 3,000 sold. Model S: Consumer Reports' highest-ever rating, Motor Trend and Automobile Car of the Year 2013. Over 20,000 sedans sold in 2013.
cases.result_zh: Roadster：10.9 万美元，售出 3000 辆。Model S：《消费者报告》史上最高分，2013 年 Motor Trend 与 Automobile 年度车。2013 年售出超 2 万辆轿车。
cases.after_state: Tesla proved cleantech's failure was about bad companies, not a bad thesis. The world does need clean energy — it just needs companies that can build monopolies.
cases.after_state_zh: 特斯拉证明清洁技术失败是公司差，非论题差。世界确实需要清洁能源——只是需要能建垄断的公司。
hard_numbers_zh: 4.65 亿美元 — 2010 年 1 月 DOE 贷款
10.9 万美元 — Roadster 价格
3000 — Roadster 销量
2 万+ — 2013 年 Model S 销量
7/7 — 七问全对

quotes.text: If you're at Tesla, you're choosing to be at the equivalent of Special Forces.
quotes.text_zh: 如果你在特斯拉，你选择的是相当于特种部队。
quotes.speaker: Elon Musk

quotes.text: Tesla's success proves that there was nothing inherently wrong with cleantech.
quotes.text_zh: 特斯拉的成功证明清洁技术本身没有错。
quotes.speaker: Peter Thiel

cases.source_excerpt: Tesla is one of the few cleantech companies started last decade to be thriving today... Tesla started with a tiny submarket that it could dominate: the market for high-end electric sports cars.
cases.source_excerpt_zh: 特斯拉是上十年创立的少数仍蓬勃发展的清洁技术公司之一……特斯拉从能主导的微小细分市场起步：高端电动跑车。
